title: Topic 425: Why True Crime Games Are Controversial: Ethics of Entertainment
content:
# Topic 425: Why True Crime Games Are Controversial: Ethics of Entertainment

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the murky, fascinating, and occasionally hilarious world of true crime video games. We explore the ethical minefield of turning real-life tragedy into interactive entertainment, a practice that has become as popular as it is problematic. By examining the psychological drivers behind our collective obsession with the macabre, and analyzing the controversies surrounding games that let us play detective (or, more disturbingly, perpetrator), we seek to understand why we are so drawn to the digital darkness. This paper argues that while true crime games may be a cathartic way to confront our fears, they also raise serious questions about our desensitization to violence and the commodification of suffering. We promise a journey that is as intellectually stimulating as it is uncomfortably funny, much like the games themselves.

## INTRODUCTION

There’s a peculiar corner of the entertainment world where the grim reality of human cruelty meets the frivolous joy of interactive play. It’s a place where you can solve a gruesome murder before your morning coffee, or step into the shoes of a notorious serial killer for a few hours of escapism. This is the world of true crime video games, a genre that has quietly grown into a cultural phenomenon, and a subject of intense ethical debate. How did we get here? When did we, as a society, decide that the most horrific acts of violence could be repackaged as a fun weekend activity?

This paper is not here to wag a finger at those who enjoy these games. After all, who among us hasn’t been captivated by a good mystery, or felt a morbid curiosity about the darker side of human nature? We’re not judging you for wanting to solve the Black Dahlia case in your pajamas. Instead, we aim to explore the complex and often contradictory reasons behind our fascination with true crime games. We will examine the psychological theories that attempt to explain this phenomenon, from our primal need to understand threats (and how to avoid becoming the subject of a future true crime podcast) to our modern-day desire for a sense of control in a chaotic world. We will also confront the uncomfortable ethical questions that arise when real-life tragedies are turned into commercial products. Is it possible to create a true crime game that is both entertaining and respectful to the victims? Or is the very idea of a “fun” murder mystery an oxymoron, like “jumbo shrimp” or “business ethics”?

Through a blend of academic analysis, real-world examples, and a healthy dose of gallows humor, this paper will navigate the treacherous terrain of true crime gaming. We will dissect the controversies, celebrate the creativity, and ultimately, try to understand what our love for these games says about us. So, grab your detective hat and your moral compass (you’ll need both), as we embark on an investigation into the ethics of entertainment.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been blind to our collective obsession with the morbid. Researchers have been diligently studying our fascination with true crime for years, and their findings are as fascinating as the crimes themselves. A prevailing theory, as noted by scholars like Dr. Scott Bonn, is that our interest in true crime is a form of “morbid curiosity,” a natural human instinct to understand the forbidden and the dangerous from a safe distance. It’s like poking a dead thing with a stick, but with a controller, a high-definition screen, and a bag of Cheetos. This isn't a new phenomenon, of course. Public executions used to be a popular form of entertainment, and the Victorian era had a strange obsession with death photography. We've just found a more technologically advanced, and arguably more comfortable, way to indulge our ghoulish side.

When it comes to video games, the literature gets even more interesting. The history of controversial video games is a long and storied one, with titles like *Mortal Kombat* and *Grand Theft Auto* sparking moral panics and congressional hearings. These games, with their graphic violence and celebration of antisocial behavior, paved the way for the true crime games of today. They normalized the idea of interactive violence, and in doing so, opened a Pandora’s box of ethical questions that we are still grappling with. It's a bit like how the invention of the automobile led to traffic jams and road rage. Progress always comes with a price, and in this case, the price is a generation of gamers who are unfazed by the sight of a virtual dismemberment.

More recent studies have focused on the specific psychological effects of playing violent video games. While the debate over whether video games cause real-world violence is far from settled, there is a growing body of research that suggests a link between playing violent games and a desensitization to violence. A study published in the *Journal of Experimental Social Psychology* found that participants who played a violent video game were slower to react to images of real-world violence, suggesting a blunting of their emotional response. It seems that after a few hours of virtual mayhem, the real thing just doesn’t have the same impact.

Furthermore, the concept of “parasocial relationships,” typically used to describe the one-sided bonds we form with celebrities, has been applied to our fascination with criminals. We become so engrossed in their stories that we feel like we know them, a phenomenon that is amplified in the interactive world of video games. This can lead to a dangerous blurring of the lines between entertainment and reality, where real-life tragedies are reduced to just another level in a game.

## ANALYSIS

So, what is it about true crime games that makes them so compelling, and so controversial? The answer lies in a complex interplay of psychological needs, game design choices, and cultural trends. At its core, the appeal of true crime games is rooted in our fundamental desire for control. In a world that often feels random and dangerous, these games offer a sense of order and predictability. We can solve the crime, catch the killer, and restore justice, all from the comfort of our living room. It’s a power fantasy for the anxious and the powerless, a way to feel like a superhero without having to wear spandex or deal with the existential dread of having to save the world before breakfast.

This desire for control is amplified by the interactive nature of video games. Unlike a book or a movie, a game allows us to actively participate in the narrative. We are not just passive observers; we are the protagonists, the detectives, the ones who make the crucial decisions. This sense of agency is incredibly seductive, and it’s what makes true crime games so immersive and, at times, so addictive.

However, this very interactivity is also what makes these games so ethically fraught. When we step into the shoes of a detective, we are also, in a sense, stepping into the world of the victim. We are exploring the scene of their death, examining their personal belongings, and piecing together the last moments of their life. This can be a deeply unsettling experience, and it raises questions about our right to intrude on the private grief of others for the sake of our own entertainment. It's one thing to read about a murder in a newspaper; it's another thing entirely to be rummaging through the victim's virtual underwear drawer, looking for clues. The line between respectful inquiry and ghoulish voyeurism is a thin one, and it's a line that true crime games often trample over with gleeful abandon.

Game developers are not oblivious to these ethical concerns. Many true crime games attempt to navigate this minefield by focusing on the puzzle-solving aspect of the investigation, rather than the gruesome details of the crime. They use stylized graphics, abstract representations of violence, and a focus on deductive reasoning to create a sense of intellectual challenge, rather than visceral horror. But even with these precautions, the fact remains that these games are based on real-life tragedies, and there is no easy way to sanitize the suffering of others.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The history of video games is littered with examples of titles that have pushed the boundaries of taste and decency. The *Grand Theft Auto* series, for instance, has built an empire on the back of controversy, with each new installment seemingly trying to outdo the last in terms of its depiction of violence and criminality. While not strictly “true crime” in the sense that they are not based on specific real-world cases, the *GTA* games have been instrumental in normalizing the idea of playing as a criminal and engaging in a wide range of illicit activities, from carjacking to murder. It’s the American dream, if the American dream involved a lot more explosions and a complete disregard for traffic laws.

The *Manhunt* series took this a step further, casting the player as a death row inmate forced to participate in a series of snuff films. The game was so brutal that it was banned in several countries and was even (falsely) linked to a real-life murder. *Manhunt* and its sequel are prime examples of the “murder simulator” subgenre, games that revel in their own depravity and seem to exist solely to shock and offend. It’s the kind of game that makes you want to take a long shower after playing, and maybe call your mom to tell her you love her.

More recently, we have seen the rise of games that are directly inspired by real-life crimes. The game *This is the Police* puts the player in the shoes of a corrupt police chief, and while it is a fictional story, it draws heavily on real-world issues of police brutality and systemic corruption. The game was praised for its complex moral choices and its willingness to tackle difficult subject matter, but it also drew criticism for its cynical and often flippant tone. It’s a game that asks the tough questions, like “How much of a bribe is too much of a bribe?” and “Is it okay to frame a guy if he’s really, really annoying?”

These examples highlight the central tension at the heart of the true crime game genre. On the one hand, these games can be powerful tools for exploring complex social issues and for forcing us to confront uncomfortable truths about our society. On the other hand, they can also be exploitative and insensitive, turning real-life tragedies into a form of cheap entertainment.

## DISCUSSION

The popularity of true crime games is not just a passing fad; it is a reflection of a broader cultural shift in how we consume and interact with media. We are no longer content to be passive consumers of information; we want to be active participants, to have a say in how the story unfolds. This desire for interactivity has fueled the rise of everything from choose-your-own-adventure novels to immersive theater, and it is the driving force behind the success of true crime games. We don't just want to watch the movie anymore; we want to be in the movie, and we want to be the one who decides whether the hero cuts the red wire or the blue wire. (It's always the red wire, by the way. Always.)

But what does this mean for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits? For one, it suggests that we are becoming increasingly desensitized to violence. The more we are exposed to graphic images of violence, whether in the news, in movies, or in video games, the less of an impact it has on us. This is not to say that we are all becoming psychopaths, but it does suggest that our collective moral compass may be in need of a recalibration. It's like when you eat so much spicy food that you can no longer taste the subtle flavors of a delicate soufflé. Our moral taste buds are being burned out by a constant diet of virtual carnage.

Furthermore, the commodification of true crime raises questions about our relationship with suffering. When we can buy and sell stories of real-life tragedy, when we can turn the worst moments of someone’s life into a form of entertainment, what does that say about our capacity for empathy? Are we losing our ability to connect with the suffering of others on a human level? Or are we simply finding new and more interactive ways to process our own fears and anxieties? It's a bit like whistling past a graveyard, but instead of whistling, we're mashing buttons on a controller and trying to get a high score.

These are not easy questions to answer, and there are no simple solutions. But as true crime games continue to grow in popularity, it is a conversation that we need to have. We need to think critically about the media we consume, and to ask ourselves whether our entertainment choices are coming at the expense of our humanity.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, the controversy surrounding true crime games is not really about the games themselves. It’s about us. It’s about our fascination with the dark side of human nature, our desire for control in a chaotic world, and our complicated relationship with violence and suffering. These games are a mirror, reflecting our own anxieties, our own morbid curiosities, and our own ethical blind spots. And like any mirror, it can be a bit unflattering at times, especially in the harsh light of day.

So, are true crime games a harmless form of entertainment, or a dangerous desensitizing force? The answer, like most things in life, is probably somewhere in the middle. They can be both. They can be a cathartic way to confront our fears, and a callous way to exploit the suffering of others. They can be a powerful tool for social commentary, and a cheap form of shock value. They are the Schrödinger’s cat of the entertainment world: both good and bad until you open the box and play the game.

Ultimately, the responsibility lies with us, the players. We are the ones who choose what to play, and we are the ones who decide how to interpret what we see. We can choose to engage with these games in a thoughtful and critical way, to use them as a starting point for a conversation about the complex ethical issues they raise. Or we can simply treat them as a mindless form of entertainment, another distraction in a world that is already full of them. The choice, as they say, is yours. Just try not to get any virtual blood on the carpet.
references:
[1] Bonn, S. (2014). *Why We Love Serial Killers: The Curious Appeal of the World’s Most Savage Murderers*. Skyhorse Publishing.

[2] Gentile, D. A., & Anderson, C. A. (2003). Violent video games: The newest media violence hazard. In D. A. Gentile (Ed.), *Media violence and children* (pp. 131-152). Praeger.

[3] Kirsh, S. J. (2006). *Children, adolescents, and media violence: A critical look at the research*. Sage Publications.

[4] Vicario, M. D., et al. (2016). The spreading of misinformation online. *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*, *113*(3), 554-559.
